title: podman auto-update(.service) fails for containers in user directory.
body:
### Issue Description

podman audo-update and podman-auto-update.service fails to restart services that have to be started using the systemctl --user flag.

The update works, but services placed in .config/containers/systemd are error with

`* restarting unit beebop.service during rollback: Unit beebop.service not found`.

Containers placed in the system-wide directory work as expected. 

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Put a .container file containing AutoUpdate=registry in the user directory under .config (also works for root). Make sure it is running properly
2. run systemctl [--user] start podman-auto-update.service 
3. If there is an update, it will download but it will fail to restart. 


### Describe the results you received

I received the error mentioned above, and the container failed to restart since it didn't find the container, presumably by not telling systemctl to start the service as a  --user.

### Describe the results you expected

The same result as a container run without --user: that the image would update and the service would be restarted.

### podman info output

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

The only reason I noticed is that I have friends with accounts on this server. One of them brought it up. It is probably not a common setup with several users running their own containers. 

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
Thanks for the report.  Can you try without `--user` but by logging into the user (e.g., via ssh)?  I wonder whether there's some implication by `--user`.

Cc: @ygalblum @alexlarsson

Trying without --user tries to start a system service and won't find the service file. That is what I suspect the podman-auto-update.service is doing (I have only tried this as root, but have the same error for files under /root/.config/containers/systemd )

It won't work at all for non-root-users.
Yes, I am not sure --user will work for this purpose.  That's why I'd like to try to login as the non-root user and run it from there with proper login session.

> Yes, I am not sure --user will for this purpose. That's why I'd like to try to login as the non-root user and run it from there with proper login session.

The same thing as it does for the root account, except it asks for auth first.

`==== AUTHENTICATION COMPLETE ====
Failed to restart beebop.service: Unit beebop.service not found.`

Restarting it with systemctl --user restart beebop.service works fine.

Apologies, I was in a haste last week and didn't think clearly.  For sure, --user is supposed to work.

Does a manual `systemctl --user restart beebop.service` work?  Same for `systemctl stop` and `start`?  It's odd that the service isn't found.  What happens when you run `podman auto-update` manually?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

So, a manual restart of a user service works fine. Everything works as expected. The only thing is that there is no auto update of user images for services defined in the users systems container dir. The auto updater works fine for every podman container that can be started using systemctl start beebop.container - i.e. a container in the system directory. 

The podman auto updater only works for those systemd units. If the units are placed in a home dir it will not work because it it will try to restart those units as if they were a system unit.

In the example in my initial post: ```systemctl --user restart beebop.service``` works. ```sysytemctl restart beebop.service``` does not since the service is not in the system wide directory. This is expected. What is not expected is the auto updater not recognizing this.
@ygalblum could this be an ordering/dependency issue where the auto-update.service runs before the Quadlets are generated?

> [@ygalblum](https://github.com/ygalblum) could this be an ordering/dependency issue where the auto-update.service runs before the Quadlets are generated?

I don't think so, because running podman auto-update fails as well. (Tried with root just now). 

Does it try to restart user services when logged in as root or does it assume they are in the system directory?

That seems like the case, because now podman auto-update works as a non-root user.

Perhaps It should trust the other users of my server and verify their claims better next time...

The issue for root still stands though.
Good news.  So the rootless/user auto-update.service works but the rootful/system service fails?
Not only that, ```podman auto-update``` fails if the containers are started from the .config/containers/systemd dir.

Can confirm this issue. Podman auto-update does not work for quatlets in ~/.config/containers/systemd/(*.container).

The services work as expected, but `podman autoupdate` does not.

The problem is that the restart (or stop and start) command forgets to use the --user flag. It then signals that the service file cannot be found and fails.

I have not succeeded using the auto update service, but I am running all my containers as root which might make a difference. I have not tested starting the auto update service with the --user flag. 

So: the auto update functionality is unaware of containers running from the user (~/.config/containers/systemd) directory - at least for the root user from what I have confirmed.
Is there already a update about this issue?
Running the auto-update service with --user does not help.

I had this issue with legacy `podman-compose@.service` when running as root. The solution was to move the unit file to `/etc/systemd/system/podman-compose@.service` and restart the containers via systemcl without `--user`
@InvisibleRain , obviously that will "solve" the issue, but that's the whole point of this issue.
